title: Interplay of Nonstabilizerness and Ergotropy in Quantum Batteries
abstract: Nonstabilizerness plays an essential role in an efficient simulation of quantum systems on quantum computers. In this work, we investigate its role in the context of quantum batteries (QBs). To this end, we consider a system of N spin-1/2 particles, where the left half serves as the charger and the right half acts as the battery. By studying different classes of interactions between the charger and the battery, we quantify the amount of nonstabilizerness required to store work in the QB. Our results reveal that a one-to-one correspondence between the ergotropy stored in the battery and the total nonstabilizerness of the composite system emerges whenever the interaction Hamiltonian preserves a U(1) symmetry. In contrast, this correspondence is generally lost for more generic interactions that do not respect this symmetry. Finally, we examine the complementary scenario in which the battery is initialized in a nonstabilizer state and subsequently charged through Clifford evolution. In this case, we find that the maximum average charging power exhibits a non-monotonic dependence on the initial nonstabilizerness. Remarkably, the highest average power can be achieved even when the initial state carries no magic (nonstabilizerness), demonstrating that the initial magic is not a necessary resource for generating an optimal charging power in this protocol.
summary: This paper explores how the complexity of a quantum state, measured by its nonstabilizerness, relates to its capacity to store extractable work in a quantum battery. It finds that while a direct link exists under U(1) symmetry, this relationship disappears in more generic, symmetry-breaking dynamics. The study also demonstrates that high initial complexity is not required to achieve maximum charging power.
main_problem: The paper investigates the relationship between nonstabilizerness (magic) and the ability of quantum batteries to store and extract work (ergotropy).
main_result: A one-to-one correspondence between ergotropy and nonstabilizerness emerges in U(1)-symmetric interactions, whereas this link breaks in generic interactions; additionally, initial nonstabilizerness is not a necessary resource for optimal charging power.
method: The authors use Stabilizer Renyi Entropy (SRE) to quantify nonstabilizerness and perform numerical simulations using XXZ spin chains, cSYK models, and random unitary circuits.
model_or_system: A 1D chain of N spin-1/2 particles divided into a charger and a battery, utilizing interaction models such as the XXZ spin chain, the complex Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (cSYK) model, and brick-wall random unitary circuits.
key_observables: Ergotropy (extractable work), nonstabilizerness (Stabilizer Renyi Entropy), stored energy, and charging power.
important_parameters: U(1) symmetry vs. symmetry-breaking interactions, system size N, circuit depth t, and the anisotropy parameter gamma.
main_assumptions: The analysis focuses on specific classes of unitary dynamics and assumes the use of SRE as a proxy for the complexity of the quantum state.
figures_summary: Figures show the growth and saturation of SRE and ergotropy over time, parametric plots showing functional relations (like tanh fits) between SRE and ergotropy, and data collapse of scaled quantities across different system sizes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of nonstabilizerness in quantum batteries, analyzes U(1)-symmetric models (XXZ, cSYK), explores symmetry-breaking interactions via random circuits, and concludes by examining the impact of initial nonstabilizerness on charging power.
why_interesting: It connects the resource theory of magic/nonstabilizerness to quantum thermodynamics, providing new insights into how quantum complexity affects the efficiency of energy storage and extraction in many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the quantum battery (QB) and charger setup. We consider a one-dimensional chain of spin- 1

FIG. 2. (a) SRE (M2), (b) ergotropy (E) and (c) work (W) stored in the battery is plotted against time, t. (a) We plot SRE of the total system consisting of battery and the charger which grows as tα with α ≈0.9 beyond the perturbative limit and saturates to a finite value. (c) Similar growth behavior in the battery is observed due to energy transfer from the charger to the battery. (b) On the other hand ergotropy remains zero initially and growth of ergotropy is observed after a critical time. (d) M2 is plotted against E for different system sizes. A one-to-one correspondence is obtained at earlier times independent of system size. Other parameters are J = 1, light to darker shades that...

FIG. 3. Average SRE is plotted against average ergotropy. We plot the SRE, averaged over the entire time evolution as a function of time averaged ergotropy for different system sizes. We segregate time and the system sizes and we obtain a non-linearly increasing SRE as a function of ergotropy, depicting storing on average more ergotropy requires more amount of average SRE.

FIG. 4. SRE is plotted against time and ergotropy while the cSYK is being the interaction between the charger and the battery. (a) We plot SRE (left axis) and ergotropy (right axis) with time while the evolution is governed by the cSYK model where both the quantity increases with time and saturates to a finite value. (b) We plot the SRE against ergotropy and find that M2 can be fitted as a function of ergotropy as A tanh(BE) + C tanh � DE2 . (d) A scale-invariant connection between SRE and ergotropy is obtained while we plot scaled SRE with ergotropy and all the data-points are on top of each other and a master-curve is obtained. All axes are dimensionless.

FIG. 5. SRE and ergotropy is plotted for a U(1)-symmetric brick-wall circuit. (a) SRE is plotted in left-axis while ergotropy is plotted on right-axis. Both SRE and ergotropy saturates to a finite value which matches with Fig. 4(a). The initial growth of both resources are different from cSYK. (b) A normalized SRE is plotted against normalized ergotropy. An one-to-one behavior is observed and SRE is fitted as ∼a tanh(bEc) with the ergotropy. All axes are dimensionless.
